title: Accordion fold insert for a traveller's notebook
subtitle: Cutting and folding a multi-page booklet to fit inside a cover
excerpt: A traveller's notebook insert is a small accordion-fold booklet held inside a leather or fabric cover by an elastic band. Making your own sets the page count, paper weight, and size. The construction uses one continuous strip folded into equal panels, with an optional card cover glued to the first and last pages.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from general booklet-insert conventions used in the traveller's notebook community and from standard accordion-structure bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A journal format in which a leather or fabric cover holds one or more booklet inserts in place with elastic bands. Inserts are replaced independently of the cover when full.

content.text: format separates cover from content: the cover is permanent; the inserts are replaceable. Making your own inserts lets you choose paper that suits what the journal is for: smooth cartridge for pen work, textured paper for watercolour washes, plain printer paper for quick sketching. The accordion fold insert needs no sewing and no adhesive binding, only cutting and folding.

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Always cut against a metal ruler, not a plastic one. A craft knife will ride over a plastic edge and veer off the cut line. Keep fingers behind the ruler edge throughout the cut. Replace blades when they drag rather than slice cleanly: a blunt blade requires more force and is more likely to slip.

items.name: Paper for pages, 80-100 gsm
items.description: Standard 80 gsm copier paper folds cleanly and works well for writing. For a heavier feel, use 100 gsm cartridge. The strip must be long enough for all panels; tape sheets end to end if necessary.

items.name: Card stock for cover, 240-300 gsm
items.description: One piece cut to the panel height and twice the panel width. This forms both front and back cover when folded in half.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For scoring fold lines before folding and pressing each crease flat afterwards. Sharp, clean folds give the insert its finished appearance.

content.text: Standard traveller's notebook sizes are passport (110 mm wide, 210 mm tall) and regular (120 mm wide, 210 mm tall). Measure the interior of your cover and subtract 2-3 mm from each dimension so the insert fits without buckling. Strip length equals the number of pages multiplied by the panel width. A 16-page insert at 110 mm width needs a strip 1760 mm long: roughly four A4 sheets joined end to end along their short edges.

Cut each sheet to the correct height. Join sheets end to end with a 5 mm overlap; apply double-sided tape on both sides of the join for a flat, invisible seam. Position joins away from fold lines where possible. The join will sit in the middle of a page, so centre it within a panel rather than placing it at the panel edge.

Mark fold lines at equal intervals along the strip. Score each line with the bone folder against the metal ruler before folding. Score firmly enough to compress the paper fibres without cutting through. Fold the strip in alternating directions: valley fold, mountain fold, valley fold: compressing each crease flat with the bone folder as you go. The completed block should stack neatly with all edges aligned.

Fold the card cover piece in half and press the spine crease firmly. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA glue to the inside face of the front cover and press the first accordion panel against it with all edges aligned. Repeat for the back cover and the last panel. Place the finished insert under a flat board with a light weight for 30 minutes until the adhesive sets. The insert then slides under the elastic band of the notebook cover.
